title: LeNet-5: The First Famous CNN
concept: LeNet-5 was an early breakthrough CNN that showed stacked convolution and pooling layers could recognize handwritten digits reliably enough to matter in real systems.

exercises.prompt: Which image task sounds most like a good early win for a small CNN?
exercises.explain: Right — a single handwritten digit is a compact image with one main thing to classify.

options.text: Telling whether a small grayscale image shows a 0-9 digit
Describing every object in a busy city street
Predicting tomorrow's weather from a selfie

cards.title: One small digit model changed the story
cards.body: Before famous deep vision systems, researchers still had to prove that layered convolution ideas could solve real image tasks. LeNet-5 became one of the first big proof points.

cards.title: What LeNet-5 was
cards.body: LeNet-5 was an early convolutional neural network built for handwritten-digit recognition. Its recipe stacked convolutions, pooling/subsampling, and a small dense classifier head so the network could turn pixels into one of 10 digit classes.

cards.title: From strokes to symbol
cards.body: Think of LeNet like a reader who first notices tiny pen strokes, then combinations of strokes like loops or corners, and finally the whole symbol. That step-by-step buildup is the CNN idea in action.

cards.title: A digit goes in, a class comes out
cards.body: A small grayscale image of a handwritten 7 enters the network. Early filters react to short edges, later layers combine those clues into a larger digit pattern, and the final head outputs scores for 0 through 9.

cards.title: LeNet was not just memorizing pictures
cards.body: It is tempting to imagine an old model as a fancy lookup table. But LeNet mattered because it learned reusable visual patterns with shared filters, which is much more flexible than storing one rule for every possible digit image.

cards.title: Why LeNet felt new
cards.body: It replaced brittle hand-written rules with a learned visual pipeline.

left.text:
Try to hand-code stroke rules
Breaks on messy handwriting
Hard to scale

right.text:
Learn filters from data
Build from strokes to digits
Conv + pooling stack works

exercises.explain: Exactly — it showed a convolution-plus-pooling stack could solve a real recognition task instead of staying a neat idea on paper.

options.text: It proved a learned conv + pooling pipeline could recognize handwritten digits in practice
It was the first model to generate full movies from text prompts
It removed the need for training data in vision
It only worked because every digit image was identical

exercises.prompt: True or false: LeNet-5 helped show that local filters and pooling could be useful building blocks for image recognition.
exercises.explain: True — that is one of the main reasons LeNet became such a famous architecture.
Local is the key word — convolution filters focus on small local patterns before deeper layers combine them.
exercises.sentence: Early convolution filters often detect small, ___ visual patterns like edges or corners.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation best captures why LeNet felt like a breakthrough?
exercises.explain: Right — the key idea is that it turned CNN building blocks into a real working system for digit recognition.

options.text: It showed that learned filters, shrinking steps, and a classifier head could work together on a real image task
It succeeded by avoiding convolutions and using only random guesses
It mattered mainly because it stored one separate rule for every pixel pattern

exercises.prompt: A classmate says, 'Why do people still mention LeNet-5?' Which answer is best?
exercises.explain: Exactly. LeNet-5 became famous because it showed a CNN with convolution, pooling, and a dense head could actually read handwritten digits well enough to matter. That made later CNN architecture work feel much more real.

options.text: Because it was one of the first famous examples showing a CNN stack could solve handwritten-digit recognition in practice
Because it was deeper than every vision model that came after it
Because it proved image models never need pooling
Because it replaced classification with text generation
